title: Bēowulf
titleEn: Beowulf — Fitt I: The Building of Heorot (lines 53–114)
part: Fitt I · The Building of Heorot (lines 53–114)
source: Fr. Klaeber, Beowulf and the Fight at Finnsburg, 1st edition (Boston: D. C. Heath & Co., 1922), pp. 3–5, lines 53–114 (fitt I). Text follows Klaeber's printing exactly — þ, ð, æ, vowel-length macrons, circumflex on contracted vowels (orcnēâs), [brackets] for letters added by emendation, (parentheses) for conjectural restoration of fire-damaged letters, and Klaeber's rows of dots where words are lost from the manuscript. Pronunciation convention: reconstructed late West Saxon, c. 1000, following Campbell's Old English Grammar. Translations made fresh from the original.
about: Beowulf survives in a single manuscript, the Nowell Codex, which was scorched in a library fire in 1731; its edges have crumbled away, and some readings survive only in two transcripts made in 1787. What you read here is a reconstruction — Klaeber's edition of 1922 — and parts of it are educated guesswork: [brackets] mark letters editors added, (parentheses) mark letters restored where the manuscript is damaged or lost, and a few words are best guesses whose exact meaning no one knows. The notes say so where it matters. Line numbers are Klaeber's, the standard way of citing the poem.

sentences.t:
Ðā wæs on burgum Bēowulf Scyldinga,
lēof lēodcyning longe þrāge
folcum gefrǣge — fæder ellor hwearf,
aldor of earde —, oþ þæt him eft onwōc
hēah Healfdene;
sentences.l: Then in the strongholds Beowulf of the Scyldings, dear people-king, was long a while famed among folks — the father had turned elsewhere, the lord from his land —, until to him in turn awoke high Healfdene;
sentences.i: Then Beowulf of the Scyldings was long famed among the peoples in the strongholds — his father had passed from the earth — until in time high Healfdene was born to him.
sentences.n: This Beowulf is still Scyld's son, the Danish king — not our hero, who is a Geat and hasn't been born yet. ‘fæder ellor hwearf’ — ‘the father turned elsewhere' — is a quiet way of saying Scyld died; ‘him eft onwōc’ — ‘to him in turn awoke' — means a son was born to him. A dynasty passes in five lines.

sentences.t:
hēold þenden lifde
gamol ond gūðrēouw glæde Scyldingas.
sentences.l: he held, while he lived, old and war-fierce, the glad Scyldings.
sentences.i: old and fierce in war, he ruled the happy Scyldings as long as he lived.
sentences.n: ‘glæde Scyldingas’ — the ‘glad' Scyldings, prosperous under a good king; remember that word — Grendel is coming for exactly that gladness. ‘gamol ond gūðrēouw’ — ‘old and battle-grim': age has not softened Healfdene, and the poem approves.

sentences.t:
Ðǣm fēower bearn forðgerīmed
in worold wōcun, weoroda rǣswa[n],
Heorogār ond Hrōðgār ond Hālga til,
sentences.l: To him four children, counted forth, awoke into the world — to the leader of hosts: Heorogar and Hrothgar and Halga the good,
sentences.i: To that leader of armies four children in all were born into the world: Heorogar, Hrothgar, and Halga the good,
sentences.n: The dative-of-birth again: children ‘awoke into the world to him'. ‘forðgerīmed’ — ‘counted forth', in full tally. The brackets in ‘rǣswa[n]’ are Klaeber's: the manuscript reads ‘ræswa'; the dative ending is editorial. Hrothgar, the second son, is the king Grendel will torment.

sentences.t:
hȳrde ic þæt [. . . . . . wæs On]elan cwēn,
Heaðo-Scilfingas healsgebedda.
sentences.l: I heard that [. . . . . . was On]ela's queen, the War-Scylfing's neck-bedfellow.
sentences.i: and a daughter — I have heard that she [was On]ela's queen, the beloved consort of the warlike Swede.
sentences.n: A famous wound in the text: the manuscript reads only ‘hyrde ic þæt elan cwen’ — the daughter's name and several words vanished by a scribe's slip. Everything in the brackets, dots included, is editorial reconstruction. Onela is a Swedish king (‘Heaðo-Scilfingas’, of the War-Scylfings, names his royal house); from Norse sources scholars guess her name may have been Yrsa — no one knows. ‘healsgebedda’ — ‘neck-bedfellow' — an intimate old word for a wife.

sentences.t:
þā wæs Hrōðgāre herespēd gyfen,
wīges weorðmynd, þæt him his winemāgas
georne hȳrdon, oðð þæt sēo geogoð gewēox,
magodriht micel.
sentences.l: Then to Hrothgar was army-luck given, worth-honor of war, so that his friend-kinsmen eagerly obeyed him, until the young company waxed, a great youth-troop.
sentences.i: Then Hrothgar was granted success in arms and honor in war, so that his kinsmen and friends served him gladly, until the band of young warriors grew into a mighty company.
sentences.n: ‘herespēd’ — ‘army-speed': ‘spēd’ is the old sense of speed — success, prosperity (it survives in ‘Godspeed'). A king's power here is circular and social: luck brings honor, honor brings men, men bring more luck. ‘oðð’ is an old doubled spelling of ‘oð’ (‘until') that Klaeber keeps.

sentences.t:
Him on mōd bearn,
þæt healreced hātan wolde,
medoærn micel men gewyrcean . . . . . .
sentences.l: Into his mind it ran, that he would command a hall-building — a great mead-house — men to build . . . . . .
sentences.i: It came into his mind to command men to build a great hall, a mead-house . . .
sentences.n: ‘Him on mōd bearn’ — literally ‘it ran into his mind' (bearn from beirnan, ‘to run'): in this language ideas arrive on their own legs. The row of dots is Klaeber's honesty: something has dropped out of the text here — the sentence as transmitted doesn't quite construe, and the edition marks the seam instead of smoothing it.

sentences.t:
þon[n]e yldo bearn ǣfre gefrūnon,
ond þǣr on innan eall gedǣlan
geongum ond ealdum, swylc him God sealde,
būton folcscare ond feorum gumena.
sentences.l: (a greater one) than the children of men ever heard tell of, and there within deal out all to young and old, such as God gave him, except the folk-share and the lives of men.
sentences.i: a hall greater than the children of men had ever heard of; and within it he would share out to young and old everything God had given him — except the common land, and men's lives.
sentences.n: The lost words before ‘þon[n]e’ (‘than') must have said ‘greater' — the comparison survives without its first half. And note what even the most generous king cannot give away: ‘folcscare’, the people's common land, and the lives of free men. Royal giving has legal edges. The brackets in ‘þon[n]e’ are Klaeber's; the manuscript reads ‘þone'.

sentences.t:
Ðā ic wīde gefrægn weorc gebannan
manigre mǣgþe geond þisne middangeard,
folcstede frætwan.
sentences.l: Then I widely heard tell of the work commanded of many a tribe throughout this middle-yard: to adorn the folk-stead.
sentences.i: Then, as I have heard it told far and wide, the task was laid upon many a people throughout this middle-earth: to build and adorn that dwelling of the folk.
sentences.n: ‘middangeard’ — ‘middle-yard, middle-earth' — the world of men, set between heaven above and whatever lies below; Tolkien lifted the word whole. ‘Ðā ic wīde gefrægn’ — ‘then I widely heard': the poet again citing oral report as his authority, as in the prologue's ‘ne hȳrde ic’.

sentences.t:
Him on fyrste gelomp,
ǣdre mid yldum, þæt hit wearð ealgearo,
healærna mǣst; scōp him Heort naman
sē þe his wordes geweald wīde hæfde.
sentences.l: For him in time it came to pass — quickly, as men reckon — that it stood all-ready, the greatest of hall-buildings; he shaped it the name ‘Heort’, he who of his word's power widely disposed.
sentences.i: In due time — swiftly, as men count such things — it stood complete, the greatest of halls; and he whose word held power far and wide gave it its name: Heorot.
sentences.n: ‘Heort’ means ‘hart' — the stag; the hall may have worn antlers on its gables. Even naming is framed as an act of power: the name is given by ‘the one whose word had wide sway'. ‘scōp him naman’ — ‘shaped a name for it': the same verb as God shaping life, and the same word as ‘scop’, the poet. Making, naming, and poetry share one verb in this language.

sentences.t:
Hē bēot ne ālēh, bēagas dǣlde,
sinc æt symle.
sentences.l: He belied not his boast: rings he dealt, treasure at the feast.
sentences.i: He did not break his vow: he dealt out rings and treasure at the banquet.
sentences.n: A ‘bēot’ is a formal, public vow — to fail one (ālēogan, ‘to lie it away') is the deepest disgrace this culture knows. The prologue's maxim about winning followers with gifts is now royal practice: Heorot works exactly as advertised. For a while.

sentences.l: The hall towered, high and horn-wide; it abode the battle-surges, the hateful flame;
sentences.i: The hall towered up, high and wide-gabled — and awaited the surge of war, the hateful fire.
sentences.n: A famous flash-forward: in the very breath that raises Heorot, the poet tells us it will burn — not by Grendel, but in a feud the next lines glance at. The poem loves telling you the ending; suspense is not its game, doom is. ‘horngēap’ — ‘horn-spacious', wide between its gable-horns.

sentences.t:
ne wæs hit lenge þā gēn,
þæt se ecghete āþumswēoran
æfter wælnīðe wæcnan scolde.
sentences.l: nor was it yet at hand, that the edge-hate of son-in-law-and-father-in-law after slaughter-malice should waken.
sentences.i: though the time was not yet come when sword-hatred between son-in-law and father-in-law would wake out of deadly feud.
sentences.n: ‘āþumswēoran’ packs ‘son-in-law and father-in-law' into one compound: Ingeld and Hrothgar — the poem's first audience knew that story, and that Heorot burns in it. The word is partly editorial: the manuscript's ‘aþum swerian’ does not construe, and Klaeber prints the standard emendation. ‘ecghete’ — ‘edge-hate': hatred in the shape of blades.

sentences.t:
Ðā se ellengǣst earfoðlīce
þrāge geþolode, sē þe in þȳstrum bād,
þæt hē dōgora gehwām drēam gehȳrde
hlūdne in healle;
sentences.l: Then the bold-spirit in hardship suffered a while — he who waited in the darknesses — that he on each of days heard joy, loud in the hall;
sentences.i: Then the mighty demon who bided in darkness suffered torment a long while: day after day he heard the joy, loud in the hall.
sentences.n: Enter Grendel — unnamed, just ‘the ellengǣst’, the ‘bold spirit' waiting in the dark. Even that word is a puzzle: it appears nowhere else, and many scholars think it a slip for ‘ellorgǣst’, ‘spirit from elsewhere', which the poem uses of him later. What torments him is not hunger but joy — the poem is precise about this, and it is the saddest thing it tells us about him.

sentences.t:
þǣr wæs hearpan swēg,
swutol sang scopes.
sentences.l: there was the harp's sound, the clear song of the scop.
sentences.i: There was the music of the harp, and the clear song of the singer.
sentences.n: The ‘scop’ (say ‘shop') is the court poet — the poem showing us its own profession at work in the doomed hall. What he sings is a song of Creation.

sentences.t:
Sægde sē þe cūþe
frumsceaft fīra feorran reccan,
sentences.l: He spoke, who knew how to reckon the first-shaping of men from far back,
sentences.i: He who could trace the origin of mankind from far-off days told the tale:
sentences.n: ‘frumsceaft fīra’ — ‘the first-shaping of men': a Genesis in miniature, sung inside a hall that a creature of Cain's kin is about to enter. The poet has arranged his ironies with care.

sentences.t:
cwæð þæt se Ælmihtiga eorðan worh(te),
wlitebeorhtne wang, swā wæter bebūgeð,
gesette sigehrēþig sunnan ond mōnan
lēoman tō lēohte landbūendum,
sentences.l: said that the Almighty wrought the earth, the beauty-bright plain, as far as water bends about it; set, victory-glorious, sun and moon as lamps for light to the land-dwellers,
sentences.i: he sang that the Almighty made the earth, the fair bright plain the waters encircle; that He, glorious in victory, set the sun and the moon as lamps to light the dwellers in the land,
sentences.n: ‘worh(te)’ — the last letters crumbled at the folio's burned edge; the parentheses are the editors' restoration. ‘swā wæter bebūgeð’ — ‘as far as water bows around it': the old picture of the world as a bright disk ringed by sea.

sentences.t:
ond gefrætwade foldan scēatas
leomum ond lēafum, līf ēac gesceōp
cynna gehwylcum þāra ðe cwice hwyrfaþ. —
sentences.l: and adorned the earth's corners with limbs and leaves; life also he shaped for each of the kinds that move about quick. —
sentences.i: and adorned the corners of the earth with branches and leaves, and shaped life too for every kind of creature that moves alive. —
sentences.n: ‘cwice’ — ‘quick' in its old sense, alive (as in ‘the quick and the dead'). The same verb again — gesceōp, ‘shaped' — for God's making; creation in this poem is craftsmanship.

sentences.l: So the troop-men lived in joys, blessedly, until one began to frame crimes, a fiend in hell;
sentences.i: So the men of the court lived in happiness, blessed — until one began to commit crimes, a fiend of hell.
sentences.n: ‘oð ðæt ān ongan’ — ‘until one began': ‘until' is this poem's hinge; happiness lasts exactly that long, every time. ‘fēond on helle’ — ‘fiend in hell' — though Grendel lives in the fens: the phrase makes him hellish wherever he stands. The m of ‘fre(m)man’ is restored — the manuscript's letter is damaged.

sentences.l: the grim ghast was called Grendel, the famed mark-stepper, he who held the moors, fen and fastness;
sentences.i: That savage demon was called Grendel: the notorious stalker of the borderlands, who held the moors, the fen and the fastness.
sentences.n: Named at last, after sixteen lines of circling. ‘mearcstapa’ — ‘boundary-walker': one who paces the marches, the no-man's-land at the edge of the mapped world. ‘gǣst’ hovers between ‘spirit' and ‘stranger/guest' (two unrelated words that look alike) — the poet may well be punning.

sentences.t:
fīfelcynnes eard
wonsǣlī wer weardode hwīle,
siþðan him Scyppend forscrifen hæfde
in Cāines cynne —
sentences.l: the monster-kind's homeland the unblessed man warded a while, after the Shaper had proscribed him among Cain's kin —
sentences.i: The unblessed creature had long kept the homeland of monsters, ever since the Creator condemned him among the kin of Cain —
sentences.n: ‘wer’ — a ‘man'! The poem lets Grendel be disturbingly human when it chooses. ‘forscrifen’ — ‘proscribed', condemned by decree (literally ‘written against'). The manuscript here reads ‘caines' altered from ‘cames’ — a scribe first wrote Ham (Cham) for Cain, a common medieval confusion; the correction is in the manuscript itself.

sentences.t:
þone cwealm gewræc
ēce Drihten, þæs þe hē Ābel slōg;
sentences.l: that killing the eternal Lord avenged, for that he slew Abel;
sentences.i: the eternal Lord avenged that murder, because Cain had slain Abel.
sentences.n: The poem assumes you know Genesis: the first murder, and the exile that answered it. Grendel's entire existence is that story's aftermath.

sentences.t:
ne gefeah hē þǣre fǣhðe, ac hē hine feor forwræc,
Metod for þȳ māne mancynne fram.
sentences.l: He got no joy of that feud, for He drove him far away — the Measurer, for that crime — from mankind.
sentences.i: Cain had no joy of that feud: for his crime the Lord drove him far from mankind.
sentences.n: ‘fǣhð’ is the technical word for blood-feud — Cain's murder is imagined in Germanic legal terms, as a feud with God Himself. ‘Metod’ — ‘the Measurer' — an old name of God: the one who metes out.

sentences.t:
þanon untȳdras ealle onwōcon,
eotenas ond ylfe ond orcnēâs,
swylce gīgantas, þā wið Gode wunnon
lange þrāge; hē him ðæs lēan forgeald.
sentences.l: Thence awoke all the mis-broods: ettins and elves and orc-corpses, likewise the giants who strove against God a long while; He paid them the reward for that.
sentences.i: Out of him awoke all the misshapen broods: ogres and elves and the restless dead — giants as well, who warred against God through a long age. He repaid them what they had earned.
sentences.n: A catalogue of everything in the dark: ‘eotenas’ (ettins — ogres), ‘ylfe’ (elves — sinister here, not fair), ‘orcnēas’ (corpse-monsters; the first element is where Tolkien found his orcs), and ‘gīgantas’, a Latin loan — the giants of Genesis, drowned in the Flood. ‘hē him ðæs lēan forgeald’ — ‘He paid them the reward for that': the reward is destruction; the understatement is the point. The circumflex in ‘orcnēâs’ is Klaeber's mark for a contracted vowel.
